goal: Explain ~80-prim warehouse scene to a second-year undergrad in grouped, narrated form.
thoughts: Raw prim list overwhelms. Group into Environment / Robots / Sensors / Lights. One pedagogic sentence per group with one concrete example. Use find_prims_by_schema to identify the driveable robot (UsdPhysics.ArticulationRootAPI). Highlight one 'teachable moment' (e.g. shelves are instanced — why USD composition matters).
code:
# 1. Stage-wide summary
scene_summary()
run_stage_analysis()

# 2. Identify the driveable robot for the "point to it" question
find_prims_by_schema(schema_name="PhysicsArticulationRootAPI")

# 3. Group output for handout:
# Environment: /World/Warehouse/Floor, /World/Warehouse/Shelves (instanced x12)
#   → "The floor is a single plane. Shelves are instanced — one asset, many copies."
# Robots: /World/amr_01 (ArticulationRoot) → this is what moves
# Sensors: /World/amr_01/front_cam, /World/amr_01/lidar
# Lights: /World/DomeLight (ambient), /World/DistantLight (sun)

# Teachable moment: point out composition — shelves are refs, not copies.
failure_modes: Dumping 80 prim paths with no grouping
Skipping the articulation identification — student still asks which robot moves
Using Isaac Sim jargon (Xform, API schema) without one-line definitions
Missing that sensors are under the robot, not at world root
